response.justification: Bogdan Szabo is a highly experienced full-stack and systems engineer, but his background is almost entirely in web, mobile, and TypeScript/Node.js ecosystems. His C++ exposure is limited to roughly six months at National Instruments (2000–2001) and a bachelor's thesis project using C++ for audio synthesis — neither of which demonstrates the depth, recency, or domain specificity the role demands. There is no evidence of multi-threaded C++ systems, message-broker-based architectures in C++, MQTT, industrial protocols (Modbus, OPC UA, IEC 61850), secure development lifecycle per IEC 62443, or industrial automation domain knowledge. The candidate is a strong engineer in a different stack and domain, and would require substantial reskilling to meet even the baseline technical requirements of this position.
strengths: Broad software engineering experience spanning 30+ years with demonstrated ability to work across multiple languages and paradigms including low-latency systems (DLang async I/O at Sociomantic).

Event-driven and distributed systems experience (GISCollective open-source geo-platform on Kubernetes, microservices handling millions of HTTP requests/sec) shows architectural thinking relevant to the role's messaging requirements.
Mentorship and knowledge-sharing track record (Refactoring Dojos at NI, Selenium course, conference talks) aligns with the team mentoring responsibilities.
concerns: C++ proficiency is extremely limited and dated — roughly six months at NI in 2000–2001 and a single thesis project; no evidence of modern C++ (C++11/14/17), STL depth, or concurrent/multi-threaded C++ development.

Zero industrial automation domain experience: no industrial protocols (Modbus, OPC UA, IEC 61850, MQTT in an IIoT context), no IEC 62443 or Secure Development Lifecycle exposure, and no embedded/on-premises industrial platform work.

Career trajectory is firmly in web and TypeScript/Node.js ecosystems; the most recent decade at re:cinq and Optilyz shows no movement toward C++ or industrial systems, making this a domain and language mismatch rather than a gap that seniority alone can bridge.
